title: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Persistent Chemotherapy-Induced Alopecia Among Women With Breast Cancer.
gap_statement: Existing studies on persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) have been conducted within racially and ethnically homogeneous populations, limiting generalizability, and PCIA-related psychological distress has rarely been compared across racial and ethnic groups using harmonized measures.
framing_pattern: Multiracial/multiethnic evidence gap + harmonization gap: 'existing studies on PCIA have been conducted within racially and ethnically homogeneous populations, limiting generalizability... the psychological distress associated with PCIA has rarely been compared across racial and ethnic groups using harmonized measures' (Introduction).

methodology_choice.data_source: Bi-national prospective cohort combining the CHANCE study at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (US, 2015-2021) and the CHANCE Korea twin cohort at Samsung Medical Center (2018-2022), with standardized trichoscopy and the validated CADS distress scale.
methodology_choice.rationale: Harmonized protocols, eligibility criteria, trichoscopy equipment, and CADS measures across the two sites were used to ensure comparability across racial and ethnic groups.

key_finding: Asian women had the highest PCIA incidence (42.1%) versus White (22.2%), Black (10.0%), and Hispanic/Latino (5.1%) women (adjusted risk ratio for Asian vs White 2.29, 95% CI 1.07-4.88), and also showed the greatest increase in CADS-measured distress, especially in emotional and activity domains.
limitations: Multi-country enrollment may introduce measurement variability despite harmonization; participation may have selected appearance-conscious patients; small Black and Hispanic/Latino samples limited precision; psychosocial covariates (education, employment, mental health history) were incompletely collected; findings may not generalize to newer agents such as antibody-drug conjugates; observational design precludes causal inference.
future_work: Large-scale, multiethnic prospective cohorts with harmonized clinical, genetic, and psychosocial data; prospective characterization of ADC-related alopecia; evaluation of pharmacogenetic screening (e.g., rs1202179, CYP3A4, CYP2D6) for risk stratification; culturally tailored preventive interventions.

title: Autologous vs. implant-based breast reconstruction and long-term mental disorder risk: a retrospective cohort study emulating a target trial.
gap_statement: Prior studies comparing autologous (AR) and implant-based (IBR) breast reconstruction focused largely on short-term aesthetic or psychological outcomes within 12 months, rarely examined clinically diagnosed mental disorders as long-term outcomes, lacked rigorous control for baseline mental health, and were limited to single-center or small cohorts, leaving the long-term psychological impact poorly understood.
framing_pattern: Inconsistency across prior studies + structural limitations: 'The inconsistency across prior studies likely stems from several key limitations. First, many have focused primarily on short-term outcomes... Second, few have examined clinically diagnosed mental disorders as long-term outcomes. Third, prior analyses often lacked rigorous control for baseline mental health conditions. Lastly, existing literature tends to be limited to single-center or small cohort studies' (Introduction).
hypothesis: AR would be associated with a higher long-term risk of clinically diagnosed mental disorders compared with IBR at 1, 5, and up to 9 years after reconstruction.

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance Service (K-NHIS) nationwide database 2015-2023, with 1:3 propensity-score matching (5113 AR vs 14 738 IBR among 24 930 eligible patients).
methodology_choice.rationale: Target trial emulation with a nationwide administrative database was chosen to address prior limitations: minimal loss to follow-up, objective claims-based mental health outcomes, and population-level generalizability beyond single institutions; an exploratory radiotherapy validation analysis served as a positive control.

key_finding: AR was associated with a higher long-term risk of any clinically diagnosed mental disorder (aHR 1.13, 95% CI 1.07-1.19), with the strongest signal in anxiety (aHR 1.25) and a more pronounced effect in patients aged >=50 years (aHR 1.16); risk persisted through 5 years, while delayed AR in younger patients showed a reversed direction (aHR 0.79, interaction p=0.021).
limitations: Observational design cannot prove causation despite target trial emulation and PS matching; psychiatric outcomes defined by administrative claims; residual confounding from unmeasured psychosocial variables (marital status, baseline psychosocial functioning, chronic pain, lymphedema); reconstruction type defined by procedure codes only; findings may not generalize beyond Korea.
future_work: Integration of mental health screening and age-sensitive, individualized counseling into preoperative decision-making; replication in other healthcare systems.

title: Quality of life, function, and symptom trajectory patterns following salivary gland cancer treatment: a 5-year prospective cohort study.
gap_statement: Longitudinal patterns of patient-reported quality of life (QoL) remain poorly understood in patients with salivary gland cancer (SGC), and baseline characteristics associated with adverse recovery patterns have not been well characterized.
framing_pattern: Long-term PRO trajectory gap in a rare cancer: 'Longitudinal patterns of patient-reported quality of life (QoL) remain poorly understood in patients with salivary gland cancer' (Abstract/Introduction).

methodology_choice.data_source: Prospective single-center cohort at a tertiary cancer center from 2016, with 252 histologically confirmed SGC patients followed for up to 5 years using EORTC QLQ-C30 and H&N35 questionnaires; group-based trajectory analysis applied.

key_finding: Three distinct global QoL trajectories were identified - Stable Good (52.4%), Declining (16.2%), and Delayed Recovery (31.3%) - with female sex, older age, worse baseline pain, and recurrence predicting unstable trajectories, while treatment intensity was not a significant predictor.
future_work: Trajectory-based classifications may support early identification of vulnerable survivors and inform risk-stratified supportive care targeting pain, nutrition, and emotional function.

title: Association of maternal pre-existing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and neonatal and long-term offspring outcomes: a nationwide mother-child paired cohort.
gap_statement: Little is known about the effects of maternal pre-existing ASCVD on offspring outcomes beyond the neonatal period; most existing research has focused on short-term obstetric complications or maternal outcomes, with few studies examining long-term developmental risks, and prior studies have been limited by small sample sizes and lacked linkage between maternal cardiovascular history and child health data.
framing_pattern: Long-term offspring outcome gap + data-linkage gap: 'little is known about the effects of maternal ASCVD on offspring outcomes, particularly beyond the neonatal period... prior studies have often been limited by small sample sizes and have lacked linkage between maternal cardiovascular history and child health data' (Introduction).
hypothesis: Maternal pre-existing ASCVD adversely influences both neonatal and long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes in offspring.

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance Service (K-NHIS) mother-child paired cohort 2005-2019 with follow-up through 2020; 5 461 222 live births, 3 640 815 unique mothers, with 1:4 propensity-score matching (145 315 ASCVD vs 581 260 non-ASCVD).
methodology_choice.rationale: Nationwide mother-child linkage via unique resident registration numbers was used to address prior small-sample and unlinked-data limitations; mixed-effects models accounted for within-mother correlation of repeated births.

key_finding: Offspring of mothers with pre-existing ASCVD had increased risks of major congenital malformations (aOR 1.09), NICU admission (aOR 1.19), neonatal sepsis (aOR 1.11), and neurodevelopmental disorders (aHR 1.08); risks persisted irrespective of adverse pregnancy outcomes, suggesting a direct contribution of ASCVD beyond APO-mediated pathways.
limitations: Retrospective administrative data may introduce information bias; only registered live births included so terminations/fetal loss not captured; ASCVD identified by ICD-10 codes without clinical chart verification; lacked detailed information on ASCVD medications and adherence during pregnancy; confined to Korea, limiting racial generalizability.
future_work: Replication across nations with diverse racial demographics; ethically designed prospective and interventional studies on cardiovascular medications during pregnancy; mechanistic studies on vascular, inflammatory, and pharmacologic pathways linking maternal ASCVD to offspring outcomes.

title: Statins in Type 1 Diabetes: Bridging the Evidence Gap With Target Trial Emulation.
gap_statement: Randomized trial evidence on statins for primary cardiovascular prevention in type 1 diabetes is lacking because T1D patients have been underrepresented in landmark statin trials, creating an evidence gap that current guidelines must navigate.
framing_pattern: Underrepresentation in RCT evidence + appeal for causal real-world inference: 'Bridging the Evidence Gap With Target Trial Emulation' (title); editorial framing of T1D as an RCT-evidence-gap population that warrants target trial emulation using real-world data (Editorial commentary).

methodology_choice.data_source: JACC editorial commentary on a target trial emulation study of statins in type 1 diabetes; no primary data source.

key_finding: Target trial emulation using real-world data can substitute for absent RCTs in populations such as T1D where trials are infeasible, supporting causal inference about statin benefit for primary cardiovascular prevention.
future_work: Broader application of target trial emulation to evidence-gap populations underrepresented in RCTs; standardized causal-inference frameworks for real-world cardiovascular data.

title: Real-World Evidence on Low-Dose Olanzapine (<=1.25 mg) for Personalized Antipsychotic Dosing.
gap_statement: Despite the absence of formal recommendations for olanzapine doses below 5 mg and growing real-world use of very low doses (<=1.25 mg/day) particularly in vulnerable and East Asian populations, prescription patterns, clinical acceptability, and metabolic safety of low-dose olanzapine have not been systematically evaluated.
framing_pattern: Guideline-practice gap + Asian pharmacogenetic specificity: 'Despite the absence of formal recommendations for doses below 5 mg, a growing interest in low-dose olanzapine... Notably, East Asian populations have been shown to require different antipsychotic dosing strategies compared to western populations' (Introduction).

methodology_choice.data_source: Samsung Medical Center Clinical Data Warehouse (CDW), 2002-2023, with 9565 Korean patients prescribed olanzapine; low-dose (<=1.25 mg/day) vs regular-dose comparison using time-varying exposure and age-as-timescale Cox models.
methodology_choice.rationale: Real-world CDW data were used because dose-response heterogeneity and very-low-dose use are not captured by formal guidelines or RCTs, and personalized prescribing requires empirical evidence reflecting actual practice.

key_finding: 17% of olanzapine users received low-dose (<=1.25 mg), with 95.5% using gradual tapering/escalation; low-dose use was associated with significantly lower risks of diabetes (HR 0.32, 95% CI 0.17-0.62) and dyslipidemia (HR 0.59, 95% CI 0.42-0.82), and numerically lower cardiovascular and cerebrovascular event risk.
limitations: Retrospective single-center design cannot establish causation; residual confounding possible; symptom severity, relapse, and functional improvement not systematically measured; long-term outcomes limited by small number of patients on prolonged low-dose therapy; single-center setting limits non-Asian generalizability.
future_work: Patient-reported outcomes and clinical assessments for therapeutic efficacy; larger cohorts to evaluate exposure-response over time; pharmacokinetic/pharmacogenetic studies on individualized dosing; development of dedicated 1.25 mg tablet formulations outside Japan.

title: Breast Cancer Information Grand Round for Survivorship (BIG-S) prospective cohort.
gap_statement: Few existing cohorts comprehensively integrate patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and real-world healthcare utilization data, particularly in Asian populations, and a lack of an Asian breast cancer registry comparable to those in Western settings exists despite distinct demographic (~50% premenopausal in Asia vs 15-30% in the West) and tumor-microenvironment differences.
framing_pattern: Asian-specific registry gap + PRO/EMR integration gap: 'few studies comprehensively integrate patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and real-world healthcare utilization data, particularly in Asian populations... a lack of an Asian breast cancer registry comparable to that available for Western patients exists' (Background).

methodology_choice.data_source: BIG-S prospective single-center cohort at Samsung Medical Center, recruiting newly diagnosed breast cancer patients from November 2018 (N=2,749); integrates EMR clinical data, bioelectrical impedance body composition, ICHOM-recommended PROs, and healthcare utilization records.
methodology_choice.rationale: The cohort was designed following ICHOM PRO recommendations to uniquely link clinical data, PROs, and healthcare utilization in an Asian survivorship setting that has been underrepresented in existing Western registries (Kaiser Permanente, Sporadic and Hereditary Breast Cancer, Harvard, CANTO).

key_finding: Among 2,749 participants (mean age 49.7), mean QoL improved from 55.6 at diagnosis to 68.2 at 4 years, but fatigue and insomnia persisted, sexual functioning and future perspectives declined, and healthcare utilization shifted from plastic surgery/rehabilitation early on to gynecology, family medicine, and psychiatry after 2 years - a pattern suggesting delayed recognition of psychosocial and sexual health needs.
future_work: Use of BIG-S to guide targeted interventions and tailored survivorship-care strategies, especially for Asian breast cancer survivors; integration into early identification and management of persistent symptoms (fatigue, insomnia, sexual dysfunction).

title: Scalp cooling for preventing persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia in anthracycline-treated patients: A single-arm trial.
gap_statement: Evidence on the efficacy of scalp cooling specifically for preventing persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) in anthracycline-treated breast cancer patients is limited, as most prior scalp-cooling evidence concerns acute alopecia rather than persistent hair loss and excludes anthracycline regimens.
framing_pattern: Regimen-specific preventive evidence gap: scalp cooling for PCIA in anthracycline-treated patients framed as an underexplored preventive intervention (Title/Keywords).

methodology_choice.data_source: Single-arm prospective trial of scalp cooling in anthracycline-treated breast cancer patients (Korea); no PMC full text available beyond title/keywords.

key_finding: Scalp cooling was evaluated as a preventive intervention for PCIA in anthracycline-treated breast cancer patients within a single-arm trial design (specific quantitative results not available from abstract).

title: Clinical significance of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol percentage reduction and attained levels after percutaneous coronary intervention.
gap_statement: American and European guidelines disagree on post-PCI LDL-C management (US threshold-for-intensification >=1.8 mmol/L vs European target <1.4 mmol/L), and it remains unclear whether patients who achieve >=50% LDL-C reduction with high-intensity statins but land in the 1.4 to <1.8 mmol/L zone benefit from additional lipid-lowering therapy.
framing_pattern: Inconsistency between US and European guidelines: 'discrepancies exist between the American and European guidelines regarding thresholds for treatment intensification... versus treatment goals... This difference often causes confusion for clinicians as to whether the additional intervention is necessary' (Introduction).

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance Service (K-NHIS) nationwide cohort 2010-2020 linked to biennial national health screenings; 135,877 adults with pre- and post-PCI LDL-C and 7.4-year median follow-up.
methodology_choice.rationale: K-NHIS coverage of nearly the entire Korean adult population with linked biennial health-screening LDL-C measurements enabled long-term real-world evaluation of percentage reduction and attained LDL-C thresholds that RCTs cannot easily address.

key_finding: Achieving >=50% LDL-C reduction was associated with lower MACCE risk (aHR 0.78) regardless of baseline LDL-C; among those achieving >=50% reduction, attained LDL-C of 1.4-<1.8 mmol/L (aHR 1.07) and >=1.8 mmol/L (aHR 1.12) had increased MACCE versus <1.4 mmol/L, supporting the more aggressive European target.
limitations: Retrospective design with unmeasured confounding (E-value 1.92); low use of ezetimibe/PCSK9 inhibitors due to long accrual period; dichotomized 50% reduction may oversimplify the continuous relationship; selection bias from requirement of second health screening within 3 years post-PCI.
future_work: Identification of most appropriate and effective strategies to achieve <1.4 mmol/L LDL-C after PCI; shared decision-making for the 1.4-<1.8 mmol/L group given marginal hard-outcome differences and cost of nonstatin add-ons.

title: Less harmful alternative for smoking: addressing concerns on the observational study of electronic cigarettes and cardiovascular outcomes.
gap_statement: The widespread framing of e-cigarettes as a 'less harmful alternative' to combustible smoking is challenged by observational evidence of cardiovascular harm, but methodological concerns about that evidence (confounding, exposure definition, dual use) need to be addressed before causal claims can stand.
framing_pattern: Contradiction to the 'less harmful alternative' assumption: title explicitly frames the editorial as addressing concerns about an observational e-cigarette/CV-outcomes study, contesting the harm-reduction narrative (Editorial/Comment).

methodology_choice.data_source: European Heart Journal editorial/comment on an observational study of electronic cigarettes and cardiovascular outcomes; no primary data.

key_finding: The editorial argues that observational evidence on e-cigarettes and cardiovascular outcomes should not be dismissed on methodological grounds alone, and that the 'less harmful alternative' framing requires scrutiny against accumulating real-world cardiovascular signals.
future_work: Improved observational designs (clearer exposure definitions, handling of dual use, causal-inference frameworks) and long-term cohorts to clarify cardiovascular risk of e-cigarettes.

title: Risk of Cancer in Newborns With Congenital Heart Disease and Their Mothers: A Nationwide Cohort Study.
gap_statement: Whether newborns with congenital heart disease (CHD) and their mothers have an elevated risk of cancer has not been established at a population level, despite biologic plausibility from shared developmental and possibly genetic pathways.
framing_pattern: Population-scale evidence gap in a rare comorbidity intersection: framed as a nationwide cohort letter examining whether CHD newborns and their mothers share an elevated cancer risk (Letter/Title).

methodology_choice.data_source: Circulation research letter reporting a Korean nationwide cohort study (likely K-NHIS) of newborns with congenital heart disease and their mothers; no PMC full text and no abstract available, so detail is limited to title.

key_finding: Reports population-level cancer risk among newborns with congenital heart disease and their mothers using nationwide cohort data (specific risk estimates not available from abstract).

title: Development and Validation of Facial Line Distress Scale-Glabellar Lines (FINE-GL).
gap_statement: Existing aesthetic-outcome measures for glabellar lines (photographic scales like the Facial Wrinkle Scale, Glabellar Line Scale, and Merz Aesthetic Scale) are single-item, severity-only tools that do not capture psychosocial distress, and existing facial-line PROMs (Facial Line Satisfaction Questionnaire, Facial Line Outcomes, FACE-Q) are designed for general facial lines and do not adequately capture concerns specific to the glabellar area.
framing_pattern: Instrument gap + condition-specificity gap: 'they are based on a single question and only assess the severity of lines without assessing their consequent psychological distress... existing measures are designed for general facial lines, and they may not adequately capture the unique functional and aesthetic concerns specific to the glabellar area' (Introduction).

methodology_choice.data_source: Two-phase instrument development per COSMIN and US FDA PROM guidance: qualitative interviews (n=25) and cognitive interviews (n=15) for content validation, followed by a cross-sectional survey at one tertiary hospital and two local clinics in Korea (n=198) with EFA, IRT-GRM, CFA, and test-retest analyses.
methodology_choice.rationale: COSMIN and FDA PROM-development frameworks were followed to ensure rigorous content, construct, and criterion validity for a condition-specific instrument.

key_finding: FINE-GL is a 20-item, 2-domain (appearance appraisal; distress due to GL) condition-specific PROM with good model fit (CFI 0.918, SRMR 0.05), Cronbach's alpha 0.92-0.97, test-retest ICC 0.77-0.90, and AUC 0.73 for predicting physician-rated severe GL (cut-off 50.6).
limitations: No existing GL-specific questionnaire was available for convergent-validity comparison; cross-sectional design precluded longitudinal validity/responsiveness assessment; recruitment from only two Korean local hospitals plus one tertiary site limits generalizability.
future_work: Longitudinal studies to confirm responsiveness of FINE-GL to clinical improvement or deterioration; broader multi-site validation.
